Is there a guarantee that the functions _provide_data_ and _provide_label_ will be called only once by the caller? If yes, then we don't need to make the change to the function. If no, then we'll have to explicitly call zip every time.

In [Python2, zip](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#zip) returns a list. So, the second time you call _provide\_data_ fn, the list is used again.
However, in [Python 3, zip](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#zip) it returns an iterator. The second time you call _provide\_data_ fn, the iterator throws an error since we already exhausted the iterator the first time we called _provide\_data_ fn. We, therefore, need to re-initialize via zip everytime we call _provide\_data_.

I think the reason is that for getShear() I thought it made more sense to use the python version of i, which is j.  So I wanted to point out that getShear() was g1 + 1j * g2, which is how you would build the complex object in python.  Then that idea carried over into some others like getG and getBeta.

But for these, it's probably better to just use i (like I did here), since they are all just describing the math, not python, and i is the more normal thing to use for that.

The Linfinity norm is indeed tough to optimize.  I haven't figured anything out on that.  But L1 and L2 would be doable.  

L1 is often called the minimax error if you do some google searching.  That's where you minimize the maximum deviation at any point, rather than the integrated absolute difference along the full path.  It seems that there are O(N) linear programming kinds of solutions for that.

L2 of course is what we would call an rms error.  It lends itself to algebraic manipulations, so there are definitely O(N) solutions to find the minimum L2 error.  

I suspect a good algorithm that combines both speed and robustness would be to use the L2 error here to find the best point to add back, but then compute the Linf error (aka the integral of the absolute deviation) in the outer loop when keeping track of the total error budget.  I suspect that the point that minimized the L2 error will often be the same or very close to the same point that would minimize the Linf error, so it should do very close to the same thing, but be significantly faster.

> Here we have error, because in `Organization(name='foo', description='bar')` we did not specify `title` field value explicitly, right?

Correct. You're telling nailgun to create a search using the "title" attribute, but the "title" attribute is not set.

> and at that moment it has `None` value?

Incorrect. No `title` attribute has been set at all.

```python
>>> from nailgun.entities import Organization
>>> org = Organization(name='foo', description='bar').
>>> hasattr(org, 'title')
False
>>> org.title
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'Organization' object has no attribute 'title'
```

Further example:

```python
>>> from nailgun.entities import Organization
>>> org = Organization(name='foo', description='bar')                                                     
>>> org.search_payload()
{'description': 'bar', 'name': 'foo'}
>>> org.search_payload({'name'})
{'name': 'foo'}
>>> org.search_payload({'title'})                                                                         
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/ichimonji10/code/nailgun/nailgun/entity_mixins.py", line 1012, in search_payload
    value = getattr(self, field)
AttributeError: 'Organization' object has no attribute 'title'
```

Yes, I grok that and agree. But I still feel like there could be a simpler way.

One might be to say roughly `if [ ! -f $VENV_BOOTSTRAP_VERSION ]; then echo "$BOOTSTRAP_VERSION" | awk '{print $1, 1}' > "$VENV_BOOTSTRAP_VERSION"; fi;`. Then PrevBootstrapVersion could just unconditionally check the file.

There might need to be changes to how NO_BOOTSTRAP is handled; you wouldn't want that getting written to a file. And you wouldn't want it to re-bootstrap unnecessarily if BOOTSTRAP_VERSION was "NO_BOOTSTRAP 1" (because --no-bootstrap was passed) and then control hit `if [ -d "$VENV_PATH" -a "$BOOTSTRAP_VERSION" != "$(PrevBootstrapVersion)" ]; then`.
